---
title: "caladaptaer"
subtitle: "R access to Cal-Adapt climate projections"
---

caladaptaer provides R access to the downscaled CMIP6 climate projections
hosted on the [Cal-Adapt Analytics Engine](https://analytics.cal-adapt.org/).
The data is stored as cloud optimized Zarr on AWS S3. Given a variable, model,
scenario, and time window, the package reads it through GDAL's `/vsis3/`
interface and returns a native R object, either a `stars` grid or a
`data.frame`. It requires no Python, no API key, and no authentication.

caladaptaer provides data access only. It does not model or transform the data
beyond reading and formatting, and it is not specific to any field. The objects
it returns work with the standard R geospatial and modeling stack.

## How it works

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
  classDef data fill:#F6A23C,stroke:#C77B1E,color:#1f1300,stroke-width:1px;
  classDef fn fill:#BBD8EC,stroke:#3B7DB5,color:#0f3350,stroke-width:1px;
  classDef ext fill:#EAF3FB,stroke:#3B7DB5,color:#0f3350,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray:5 3;

  subgraph DATA["Data"]
    direction TB
    S["Cal-Adapt on AWS S3<br/>WRF and LOCA2 Zarr stores"]:::data
    R["native R object<br/>stars grid or data.frame"]:::data
    D["CF-1.8 NetCDF<br/>model drivers"]:::data
    S --> R --> D
  end

  subgraph CODE["caladaptaer"]
    direction TB
    DISC["Discover<br/>cae_catalog, cae_models, cae_scenarios,<br/>cae_variables, cae_search, cae_check_variables"]:::fn
    FETCH["Fetch<br/>cae_fetch, cae_fetch_point, cae_fetch_points"]:::fn
    DRV["Convert<br/>cae_write_cf_netcdf, cae_build_met_drivers"]:::fn
  end

  DOWN["your model or analysis<br/>any field"]:::ext

  DISC -.->|"query catalog"| S
  FETCH -->|"GDAL /vsis3/"| R
  DRV --> D
  R --> DOWN
  D --> DOWN
```

The Analytics Engine stores its climate data as [Zarr](https://zarr.dev/), a
cloud native array format. A Zarr store is not one giant file you download
whole; it is split into many small chunks, and a reader can pull just the chunks
it needs over the network. caladaptaer reads those chunks through GDAL's
`/vsis3/` virtual filesystem, the same engine already sitting under `sf`,
`stars`, and `terra`. As a result there is nothing to install on the Python
side, no key to manage, and no client session to maintain. A request returns
the corresponding subset of the store as an R object.

## Available data

The Engine hosts two downscaling products, both derived from CMIP6 global
models for California and the western US.

| Dataset | Resolution | Step | GCMs | Scenarios | Period |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| WRF CMIP6 (WUS-D3) | 45 / 9 / 3 km | Hourly | 8 | SSP3-7.0 (all); SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 (CESM2) | 1980--2100 |
| LOCA2-Hybrid | 3 km | Daily | 15 | SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, SSP5-8.5 | 1950--2100 |

The two products are produced by different methods and suit different uses. WRF
is dynamically downscaled by UCLA (Rahimi et al. 2024), a physics based weather
model run at high resolution; it carries the full hourly surface meteorology and
resolves features such as mountain rain shadows and the sea breeze, and is
suited to applications that need hourly drivers or process based physics. LOCA2
is statistically downscaled by Scripps (Pierce et al.), mapping coarse model
output onto a fine grid from historical analog patterns; it provides daily data
for more models and scenarios at 3 km, and is suited to applications that need a
wide ensemble or daily extremes. caladaptaer reads both, and the ERA5 reanalysis
driven WRF run, through the same set of functions.

## Functionality

The `cae_*` functions cover the path from catalog to model ready files. The
CADCAT catalog can be searched by model, scenario, variable, and resolution, and
a coverage check reports whether a given model and scenario carry the required
fields. Data can be read as a `stars` grid, or extracted as a time series at one
or many points; the multi point read retrieves the grid from S3 once and
extracts all sites locally, so the cost is one read per variable rather than one
per site. WRF output can be converted to CF-1.8 NetCDF in a single call, with
unit conversions applied. For models that store precipitation split across
`rainc` and `rainnc`, the total is computed automatically.

## Quick start

```r
remotes::install_github("lebauerapproach/caladaptaer")
```

```r
library(caladaptaer)

# what is on the engine
cae_models(activity = "WRF")

# does CESM2 SSP3-7.0 carry the variables I need
cae_check_variables("CESM2", "ssp370")

# a summer day of hourly temperature at Fresno, 2050
cae_fetch_point("t2", model = "CESM2", scenario = "ssp370",
                lon = -119.77, lat = 36.75,
                start_time = "2050-07-01T00:00:00",
                end_time   = "2050-07-01T23:00:00")
```

[Getting Started](vignettes/getting_started.qmd) covers install through a first
time series, [Data Access](vignettes/data_access.qmd) documents every function
with live output, and [Showcase](vignettes/showcase.qmd) presents worked
analyses against live data.

## Used in production

We run caladaptaer in our MAGiC project to pull climate
projections for our California design points, where it has already generated
12,474 site-years of hourly drivers: three GCM and scenario combinations (CESM2
SSP2-4.5, CESM2 SSP3-7.0, MPI-ESM1-2-HR SSP3-7.0) over 2025 to 2045.

## Roadmap

caladaptaer currently covers data access: catalog discovery, reading Zarr
stores, extraction at one or many points, and conversion to CF-1.8 NetCDF.
Planned development includes:

- Recording provenance on each output, including the source store, version,
  time and space slice, model, and scenario, along with the relevant dataset
  citation, so a result can be reproduced and cited from the file itself.
- An analysis layer for commonly reported quantities, including spatial
  subsetting to administrative or hydrologic regions, temporal aggregation,
  derived variables such as growing degree days and reference evapotranspiration,
  warming levels, threshold and extreme statistics, and bias correction.
- Bounding box subsetting on read, to retrieve only the requested region.

Later milestones include rOpenSci review and a CRAN submission. The package is
not tied to any one field or downstream model. The legacy R client (caladaptr)
targets the previous Cal-Adapt API and CMIP5 era data rather than the Analytics
Engine's CMIP6 stores.

## Guides and reference

| Page | What is on it |
|---|---|
| [Getting Started](vignettes/getting_started.qmd) | Install to a real time series in a few calls |
| [Data Access](vignettes/data_access.qmd) | Every function, with live output |
| [Showcase](vignettes/showcase.qmd) | Worked analyses that show what the data can do |
| [SIPNET Met Drivers](vignettes/sipnet_met_drivers.qmd) | WRF to CF NetCDF for ecosystem models |
| [Bulk Met Drivers](vignettes/bulk_met_drivers.qmd) | The multi site driver path |
| [Reference](reference/index.html) | Every function, every argument |

## Citing and license

caladaptaer is released under BSD-3-Clause. See `CITATION.cff` in the repo for
how to cite it.
